---
format: 
  revealjs:
    margin: 0.05
    max-scale: 4
    fontsize: "10px"
    chalkboard: true
    theme: [default, style.scss, lecture5.scss]
    slide-number: c
    show-slide-number: all
    preview-links: auto
    footer: "&copy; 2022 Rodolfo Lourenzutti & Eugenia Yu -- Material Licensed under [CC By-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)"
---

```{python}
from IPython.display import Markdown
from tabulate import tabulate
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import altair as alt
import plotly.express as px
from vega_datasets import data
```


# Association between variables 
STAT 200 - Lecture 5

## Association

- Earlier, we explored association between categorical variables; 

- We will now extend this discussion for quantitative variables;

## Association

::: {.incremental}

- Do people with bigger brains **tend** to be more
intelligent?

- Do students with higher attendance **tend** to have better performance in a course? 

- Do taller athletes **tend** to be faster in 100m? 

- Do taller penguins **tend** to be heavier? 

:::

## Scatterplot

- In scatterplot, each observation is represented by a point;
  - *explanatory variable* goes in the $x$-axis;
  - *response variable* goes in the $y$-axis; 

- Scatterplots are really useful to visualize relationship between two quantitative variables;

## Scatterplot: Example 1

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
```{python}
cars = data.cars()

alt.Chart(cars)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = 'Horsepower:Q',
      y = alt.Y('Miles_per_Gallon', title='Miles per gallon', type='quantitative'))\
  .properties(
      title='Cars Data',
      height=300,
      width=500)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
:::{.data_table .center_text style='height:350px;'}
```{python}
Markdown(tabulate(
  cars[['Miles_per_Gallon', 'Horsepower']].to_numpy().tolist(), 
  headers=['Miles per Gallon', 'Horsepower']
))
```
:::
:::

::::

## Scatterplot: Example 2

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
```{python}
alt.Chart(cars)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = alt.X('Weight_in_lbs:Q', title='Weight (lbs)', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)),
      y = alt.Y('Acceleration:Q', title='Time from 0 to 60 mph (sec)'))\
  .properties(
      title='Cars Data',
      height=300,
      width=500)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
:::{.data_table .center_text style='height:350px;'}
```{python}
Markdown(tabulate(
  cars[['Weight_in_lbs', 'Acceleration']].to_numpy().tolist(), 
  headers=['Weight_in_lbs', 'Acceleration']
))
```
:::
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
- Correlation is weaker, as many other things affect acceleration besides weight, such as horsepower;
:::

## Scatterplot: Example 3

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
```{python}
penguins = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/allisonhorst/palmerpenguins/main/inst/extdata/penguins.csv")
penguins = penguins.dropna(axis=0)
penguins.columns = [column.replace('_mm','') for column in penguins.columns]
penguins.columns = [column.replace('_g','') for column in penguins.columns]
penguins = penguins.drop(['year'], axis=1)


alt.Chart(penguins)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = alt.X('body_mass:Q', title='Body Mass (g)', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)),
      y = alt.Y('flipper_length:Q', title='Flipper Length (mm)', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)))\
  .properties(
      title='Penguins Data',
      height=300,
      width=500)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
:::{.data_table .center_text style='height:350px;'}
```{python}
Markdown(tabulate(
  penguins[['body_mass', 'flipper_length']].to_numpy().tolist(), 
  headers=['body_mass', 'flipper_length']
))
```
:::
:::
::::

::: {.notes}
- Correlation is weaker, as many other things affect acceleration besides weight, such as horsepower;
:::

## Scatterplot: what to look for? 

**[Direction]{style='margin-left:35vh; text-decoration: bold; color: blue; margin-bottom: 0;'}**

:::: {.columns style='margin-top: 0;'}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
alt.Chart(penguins)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = alt.X('body_mass:Q', title='Body Mass (g)', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)),
      y = alt.Y('flipper_length:Q', title='Flipper Length (mm)', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)))\
  .properties(
      title='Penguins Data',
      height=350,
      width=400)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::{style='position: relative; bottom: 55px; left: 100px; margin-top:0;'}
Positive Correlation
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
alt.Chart(cars)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = alt.X('Weight_in_lbs:Q', title='Weight (lbs)', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)),
      y = alt.Y('Acceleration:Q', title='Time from 0 to 60 mph (sec)'))\
  .properties(
      title='Cars Data',
      height=350,
      width=400)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::{style='position: relative; bottom: 55px; left: 100px; margin-top:0;'}
Negative Correlation
:::
:::
::::

## Scatterplot: what to look for? 

**[Form of relationship]{style='margin-left:28vh; text-decoration: bold; color: blue; margin-bottom: 0;'}**

:::: {.columns style='margin-top: 0;'}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
alt.Chart(penguins)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = alt.X('body_mass:Q', title='Body Mass (g)', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)),
      y = alt.Y('flipper_length:Q', title='Flipper Length (mm)', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)))\
  .properties(
      title='Penguins Data',
      height=350,
      width=400)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::{style='position: relative; bottom: 55px; left: 100px; margin-top:0;'}
Linear Relationship
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
alt.Chart(cars)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = 'Horsepower:Q',
      y = alt.Y('Miles_per_Gallon', title='Miles per gallon', type='quantitative'))\
  .properties(
      title='Cars Data',
      height=350,
      width=400)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::{style='position: relative; bottom: 65px; left: 55px; margin-top:0;'}
Non-linear Relationship
:::
:::
::::


## Scatterplot: what to look for? 

**[Strength of Relationship]{style='margin-left:28vh; text-decoration: bold; color: blue; margin-bottom: 0;'}**

:::: {.columns style='margin-top: 0;'}
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
x = np.random.uniform(2500, 6500, 500)
synthetic_data = pd.DataFrame({
  'X': x ,
  'Y-strong': x / 10 + np.random.normal(0, 20, 500),
  'Y-weak': x / 10 + np.random.normal(0, 75, 500)
})

alt.Chart(synthetic_data)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = alt.X('X:Q', title='X', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)),
      y = alt.Y('Y-strong:Q', title='Y', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False, domain=[100, 900])))\
  .properties(
      title='',
      height=350,
      width=380)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::{style='position: relative; bottom: 55px; left: 65px; margin-top:0;'}
Stronger Relationship
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{python}
alt.Chart(synthetic_data)\
  .mark_point()\
  .encode(
      x = alt.X('X:Q', title='X', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False)),
      y = alt.Y('Y-weak:Q', title='Y', scale=alt.Scale(zero=False, domain=[100, 900])))\
  .properties(
      title='',
      height=350,
      width=380)\
  .configure_title(
      fontSize=20,
      anchor='middle',
      color='Black')\
  .configure_axis(
      labelFontSize=16,
      titleFontSize=17
  )
```
:::{style='position: relative; bottom: 55px; left: 80px; margin-top:0;'}
Weaker Relationship
:::
:::
::::


## The correlation coefficient

- The correlation coefficient, $r$, measures the strength of the **linear** association between two
**quantitative** variables; 

. . . 

- The correlation coefficient is always between -1 and 1:
  - [**Positive correlation**: an increase in $X$ **tends** to be followed by an increase in $Y$;]{style='font-size:.85em;'}
  - [**Negative correlation**: an increase in $X$ **tends** to be followed by an decrease in $Y$;]{style='font-size:.85em;'}


## Properties of Correlation

:::{.fragment .fade-in-then-out}
- $r$ has no unit;

- $r=-1$ for perfect negative correlation

- $r=1$ for perfect positive correlation

- $r\approx 0$ implies very weak or no linear relationship between the variables;
:::

. . .

:::{style="position: absolute; top: 17vh; background-color: white;"}

:::{.callout-important}
## Warning
$r$ close to zero **does not** imply two variables are not related. They could still have a non-linear relationship.

:::

:::

## Properties of Correlation

- Cor($X$, $Y$) = Cor($Y$, $X$)

- the correlation does not change if all values of either variable are added
a constant or multiplied by a positive constant

- $r$ is sensitive to outliers;
